Article.ArticleTitle: Effectiveness of motor imagery training in women with chronic neck pain: a single-blind, randomized controlled trial.

Abstract.AbstractText: Motor imagery training (MIT), a cognitive technique that involves mentally simulating movement, addresses the maladaptive neuroplastic changes associated with chronic pain.

The study aimed to investigate the effects of motor control exercises (MCE) combined with MIT on pain, pain-related beliefs, functional status, and neck awareness in women with chronic neck pain (CNP).

In this single-blind, randomized controlled trial, forty female participants with moderate CNP were allocated into two groups using stratified randomization: an experimental group (MCE, 30 min + MIT, 20 min; n = 20) and a control group (MCE, 30 min; n = 20). The researchers adapted the MIT procedure utilized in this study. Participants underwent training twice a week for eight weeks, with assessments conducted at pre-treatment, post-treatment, and a 12-week follow-up. Outcome measures included pain intensity, pain beliefs, muscle strength, endurance, functional status, neck awareness, and motor imagery ability.

Both the experimental (mean age 51.50 ± 9.31 years) and control (mean age 55.00 ± 11.64 years) groups exhibited significant improvements in pain, pain-related beliefs, functional status, and neck awareness scores post-treatment (p < .05), and these improvements were maintained during the follow-up period. Furthermore, the experimental group demonstrated significantly greater improvements, with moderate-to-high effect sizes, in pain behavior (p = .01, η2 = 0.08), functional status (p < .05, η2 > 0.06), and neck awareness (p = .03, η2 = 0.09) compared to the control group (p < .05, η2 > 0.06).
The combination of MIT and MCE was more effective than MCE alone in improving pain-related parameters, functional status, and neck awareness in women with CNP.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Institute of Health Sciences, Department of Neurology Physiotherapy, Hacettepe University, Ankara, Turkey.
School of Sarıkaya Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Yozgat Bozok University, Yozgat, Turkey.
Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Yozgat Bozok University, Yozgat, Turkey.
Faculty of Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Hacettepe University, Ankara, Turkey.
